does: This paper systematizes emerging biosecurity threats posed by generative AI in the biosciences through expert consultation and threat modeling. The authors identify specific vulnerability vectors including jailbreak attacks that circumvent safety guardrails, privacy leaks in training data, and dual-use risks where models could generate instructions for creating synthetic pathogens or toxins. They conducted 130 expert interviews across academia, government, industry, and policy sectors, finding that approximately 76% of respondents expressed concern about AI misuse in biology and 74% called for new governance frameworks. The work catalogs current gaps in regulatory oversight and technical safeguards, establishing a baseline understanding of where biosecurity risks concentrate as generative models become increasingly integrated into biotech and synthetic biology workflows.
connects: This work builds on longstanding dual-use research concerns in biology and the recent acceleration of AI capabilities applied to life sciences. It extends prior biosecurity frameworks developed for synthetic biology and dangerous pathogen research into the new domain of large language models and generative systems. The paper responds to emerging vulnerabilities identified in adversarial robustness literature, prompt injection attacks documented in ML safety research, and privacy attacks on language models. It draws connections to existing governance conversations around biosafety oversight, institutional biosafety committees, and export controls, while acknowledging that these mechanisms were designed before AI could dramatically lower the technical barrier to sensitive biological work. The work synthesizes insights from both traditional biosecurity experts and AI safety researchers, bridging communities that have historically operated separately.
enables: This paper lays groundwork for developing concrete technical and governance solutions to biosecurity-AI risks. It positions future work on robust content filtering specifically tuned to biological threat detection, adversarial training methods that prevent jailbreaking of biology-focused models, and new inference-time safeguards for autonomous biological reasoning systems. The expert consensus documented here creates mandate for developing binding governance frameworks, possibly including licensing, monitoring, and disclosure requirements for dual-use capable models in biology. The systematic threat taxonomy enables prioritization of mitigations, whether through model architecture choices, fine-tuning approaches, access controls, or regulatory mechanisms. It also highlights the need for continuous threat modeling as AI capabilities evolve, suggesting institutional structures like biosecurity review boards for AI-driven biology research and interdisciplinary collaboration between computer security and biological expertise.
